items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.

items.q: What does inheritance allow a subclass to do?
items.correct: Reuse and extend the attributes and methods of its superclass.
items.random_wrong: Copy code from any unrelated class.
items.why: Students think inheritance overrides everything. It actually REUSES the parent's members (which can be added to or overridden), creating an 'is-a' relationship.

items.q: What is the time complexity of binary search?
items.misconception: O(n) - because it searches through items.
items.why: Students conflate 'fast' with O(1). Binary search halves the search space each step, giving logarithmic time - much better than linear, but not constant.
Correct answer: Supervised. “Unsupervised” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Clustering. “Classification” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many main paradigms of machine learning are usually named at A-Level?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
